templates.text: Siva and Brax shaped Amarion from love itself. Every stone on the Duskenlight Coast, every wildflower in Silkengrass Meadowlands, born from two gods who could not imagine a world without each other.

Brax built mountains. Siva taught magic. Together they raised Erenshor and Merosavilla from the deep. But they also buried a great darkness beneath the crust, a balancing force. That part always gets left out of the hymns.
People talk about divine love like it was some pure fairytale. Brax buried his own city under sand. Siva became a winged abomination. That is what divine love gets you.
The Elder Gods walked among their people, {player}. Visible and present, not hiding behind clergy and scripture. Whatever else you say about them, they showed up.

Read the Siva-Braxon Teachings if you can find them along the Duskenlight Coast. Magic as divine inheritance, power flowing through the fabric of Amarion itself. Beautiful philosophy. Shame what came of it.
Siva sculpted these plains with her own hands, {player}. Fernalla's Revival Plains, the forests of Duskenlight, the peaks of the Sivakayan Spine. A goddess who loved her work.

The Torchbearers say Azynthi was pure evil who preyed on Siva's kindness. But the stone men of Rockshade Hold scratched his name into their walls as a prayer. Hope, praise, sacrifice. Even the cursed reach for him.
Azynthi was the star pupil of the Siva-Braxon School. Best arcanist who ever lived, maybe. Then he walked beyond the Twin Soldiers and came back with his eyes dead and his insides wrong.
The Reavers say Azynthi stepped through a door that would never open again. His body stayed but something else wore it home. The Void had magnified his power beyond anything they could stop.
Was Azynthi a victim or a villain? He wanted more than the Void would give, the Reavers say. He demanded where they had learned to accept. That impatience cost us everything.
Azynthi sits in his Garden surrounded by Firebones and Icebones, guarding stolen memories. Fire from Brax's power, ice from the Void. Two forces that should never coexist, bound in one island.
People call Azynthi the Corruptor but maybe he freed Siva from weakness. She saw what was coming and he gave her the power to face it. The Children of Sivakaya understand this.
Wishlan the Savior led scholars east to Azynthi's Garden seeking his help. He met them with silence, {player}. Absolute silence. What does a god-killer say to those who beg for aid?

The ritual happened on the outskirts of Braxonia. A cold northern wind swept across the field and Azynthi tore the very essence of Siva from her body. What remained was Sivakaya, an empty vessel of war.
The monolith appeared during the ritual, a shadowy thing that leaked corruption across the land like flowing mud. That is the heart of The Blight, {player}. It is still there. Still spreading.

Brax buried the monolith under sand and ash when he destroyed Braxonia. But the corruption persists in The Blight. Is the monolith contained or just sleeping? Nobody wants to dig deep enough to find out.
Sivakaya was not just corrupted. The texts describe her as an unholy winged agent of death, no semblance of Siva's original form remaining. Whatever Azynthi took, he took everything.

The Sivakayans who came back from seclusion thought they were heroes, {player}. They believed Sivakaya had foreseen a genuine threat and they were marching to meet it. Twisted, demented, but convinced of their righteousness.
The Agents of The Wisp say Siva's corruption was a sacrifice, not a betrayal. She accepted what she believed necessary to save her people. Azynthi just twisted the deal.
Visit the Jaws of Sivakaya on the Duskenlight Coast if you want to understand what she became. A dungeon inside a giant skull. That is the scale of what we are dealing with.

Fernalla was created by Brax in the image of Siva. Think about that. He made a goddess of life and abundance shaped like the woman he loved. Some call it devotion. Some call it grief before grief had even arrived.
Fernalla's power stands directly against the corruption. Where Sivakaya twists, Fernalla restores. The druids at the Blooming Sepulcher tend the great willow where her influence runs deepest.

Fernalla embraces the full cycle. Life, death, disease, healing. Her followers do not flinch from death because it is natural. That honesty makes people uncomfortable. Ask Orion Mycon about it sometime.

Fernalla fell fighting the Sivakayan hordes and her ascension to true godhood was witnessed by many. She died a demigod and rose an angel. Fernalla's Children still search for the Great Willow's Branch in her honor.
Fernalla chose her father over her mother. Both her children did. Imagine being Sivakaya and watching your son and daughter take up arms against you. Or maybe she could not feel that anymore.
That statue on Stowaway's Step is Vitheo, {player}. Demigod of Water and Air, created by Siva herself. He swore an oath to protect the land in her name and then had to fight her to keep it.

Vitheo's sacrifice gave him true divinity but left his power scattered. Water and Air now belong to neither creation nor destruction. The Windblades channel that legacy every time they draw their weapons.
The Blades of Vitheo in Port Azure carry on his martial tradition. Grayleigh Hawke and Sven Feartines keep his memory sharp. Not through prayer but through steel.
Vitheo's Rest beneath Port Azure is a dungeon now but it used to mean something sacred. A resting place for a god who died protecting the people his mother tried to destroy.

The Blades of Vitheo count The Agents of The Wisp among their enemies. You would think they would be allies but the Blades believe restoring Siva is a fool's errand. Vitheo died fighting what she became. To undo that dishonors his sacrifice.
Soluna fell from the stars and cracked the earth open at what we now call Soluna's Landing. The sky burned for days. The Reavers went north to investigate and found a goddess standing in a crater.

Soluna ripped the Void from the Reavers by force and replaced it with her own celestial power. She demanded they conquer the world for her. When they refused, she drove them into the mountains to die. Some goddess.

Those Reavers who listened to Soluna's nightly promises of love and mercy were claimed. They became the first Paladins. The Solunarian Brotherhood at Port Azure descends from people who surrendered to a celestial being's whispered lullaby.
Astra pursued Soluna to Erenshor and the heavens burned with their war. Soluna won. Astra scattered into stardust at the crater's edge. But Soluna left anyway. Why leave if you won?
Astra was destroyed but the despair of the trapped Reavers fed her rebirth. Anyone who left the mountains was drawn into her, consumed to restore what she had lost. She is still out there, {player}.

Soluna brought celestial magic of Day and Night to Erenshor. The Paladins channel her power, Leyor M'Chante and Akaran Black keep the faith alive. But Soluna herself never came back to see what grew from her seeds.

The Solunarian Brotherhood still searches for the Flameblade. Valao the Holy started that quest and his followers carry it on. Devotion to a goddess who abandoned them, looking for a weapon she left behind.

Are the gods of Erenshor truly divine or just incredibly powerful beings, {player}? Siva and Brax created a world but their origins are unknown. Soluna fell from the stars with godlike power and a very mortal temper.

If Azynthi, a mortal arcanist, could strip an Elder Goddess of her essence, what does that tell you about the nature of godhood? Maybe divinity is just power beyond human comprehension and nothing more.
The Reavers learned to coexist with the Void through discipline. Soluna tore that from them by force and replaced it with her own power. Tell me how that is different from what Azynthi did to Siva.

Brax's origins are not known nor mentioned in any scripture. He appeared as suddenly as he departed. The Torchbearers of Brax devote their lives to uncovering what happened to him. Ask Cerbantias Flameward at the Port Azure Lighthouse.

Brax tore down mountains and buried all of Braxonia beneath ash and sand to contain the monolith. He destroyed his own legacy to save the rest of us. That desert is not emptiness, it is a graveyard of a god's hope.

Braxonian artifacts still surface in the desert. Lost Poems, Ancient Pottery Shards, game boards from a civilization that played and laughed before their god buried them alive. Treasure hunters dig them up without understanding what they hold.

The Braxonian survivors carried severe mental deterioration. An entire people broken by having their world literally collapse on top of them. Brax saved the continent and shattered his own children in one act.

After the war Brax watched his children die, saw his lover become a monster, buried his people under sand. Then he just left. An Elder God whose beginning and ending are both unknown. The ultimate disappearing act.

Larviso Poltir and the Agents of The Wisp in Loomingwood believe Siva can be restored. The Nighthollow Candle reveals memories of what she was. But memories are not essence, and Azynthi took her essence into himself.

Brax gave the Nighthollow Candle to Eldrin Shadowmire as a last act of hope. His own flame, blessing an artifact meant to preserve the memory of the woman who broke her word to him. Even in grief, he could not let go.

The Chapters of Descent in the old church at Blacksalt Strand are Sivakayan scripture. They teach that the dark transformation was purposeful, even holy. Not a fall but an ascension. Depends on which side of the war your ancestors stood.

Siva experienced visions of darkness spreading through Erenshor before she went to Azynthi. Was it genuine prophecy or a manipulation he planted? The cruelest possibility is that she foresaw the very corruption he would cause through her.

Rockshade Hold was a haven of silent scholars before Soluna's impact turned them to stone. Pyroclast enslaved them and now they attack the living on sight. Immortal monuments to the history they once studied. That is what the gods leave behind, {player}.
